patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 12-year-old boy who is anxious and vague. Start by saying you keep getting bad skin and chest infections and this one isn’t getting better. patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 22-year-old female, cooperative and clear. Start by saying you bleed a long time after small cuts and get large bruises without clear injury. Early in the visit, volunteer: a tooth extraction last year oozed for days, and your parents said you bled from your belly button as a newborn. If asked about other bleeding, mention: gums bleed with brushing, very heavy periods lasting >1 week, occasional bruises that become painful lumps, bleeding that restarts 1–2 days later, and a few long-lasting nosebleeds. Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: slow-healing “damp” wounds, a past miscarriage, and after a mild head bump you later had a bad headache and unusual sleepiness. Express misconceptions: you wondered about hemophilia but don’t have swollen painful joints; routine clotting tests were “normal,” so you questioned anxiety/clumsiness; you feel lightheaded and suspect low iron. nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'gallbladder neuroendocrine tumor' and includes 'grade 1/2' in the explanation.

Communicates the next key management concepts: staging imaging and surgical management as the main potentially curative approach when resectable.
Clarifies that additional treatment decisions depend on stage (localized vs nodal/metastatic), surgical margins, and tumor extent; avoids overpromising cure.
